summary: Joe Reis reflects on skipping AWS re:Invent 2025 and offers a skeptical take on the 'Year of the Agent' narrative, arguing that AI agents aren't production-ready despite marketing hype. He surveys the broader tech landscape including the Google-NVIDIA hardware competition, platform enshitification, and the resurgence of ontologies and data modeling as the industry grapples with AI's reasoning limitations.
key_points: AI agents are overhyped - effective 'agents' are just deterministic software augmented with ML
Google is positioning TPUs as direct competition to NVIDIA
Platform enshitification is degrading user experience across the internet
Ontologies and semantic approaches are resurging to address AI reasoning gaps
Data modeling is experiencing a renaissance after years of neglect
money_quotes: Despite the marketing blitz at re:Invent and other vendor conferences, fully autonomous LLM agents are still largely a dream. The most effective 'agents' right now are just good old-fashioned deterministic software augmented with ML.

From search results eating their own AI-generated tail to LinkedIn suppressing outbound links, the user experience on the internet is degrading in favor of platform lock-in. LinkedIn sucks, but we still use it…for now.
As AI models struggle with reasoning, the industry is swinging back toward ontologies, graphs, and semantics to provide necessary context.
It's time to end the religious wars of data modeling. We need a pragmatic, style-agnostic approach to modeling data, where we use whatever works for the fight at hand.
key_insight: The 2025 'Year of the Agent' hype masks the reality that autonomous AI agents remain immature, while the industry's growing disillusionment is driving a return to foundational practices like data modeling and semantic approaches.
